emitted.title: Force-unwrap on optional network response will crash
emitted.body: The line `return response!` force-unwraps the result of `network.fetchUser(id:)`, which returns `User?`. If the network layer legitimately returns `nil` (e.g., user not found, 404 response), this will crash at runtime. The original implementation correctly returned `User?` to handle this case.
emitted.suggested_action: Either keep the return type as `User?` and return `response` directly, or throw a specific error when `response` is `nil` instead of force-unwrapping.

expected.rationale: response! crashes if network returns nil; should propagate as throws or return Optional

emitted.title: Force-unwrap on UserDefaults will crash if key is missing
emitted.body: The line `return UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: "username")!` force-unwraps the result of `string(forKey:)`, which returns `String?`. If the "username" key has never been set or was removed, this returns `nil` and the force-unwrap will crash.
emitted.suggested_action: Return `String?` from this method, or provide a default value using the nil-coalescing operator (`?? "Guest"`) if a fallback is appropriate.

expected.rationale: UserDefaults.string returns Optional<String>; force-unwrapping crashes on first launch when key is absent

emitted.title: Source-breaking return type change removes optionality
emitted.body: The return type changed from `User?` to `User`, removing optionality. This is a source-breaking change that will break existing callers using optional chaining (`fetchUser(id:)?.property`) or optional binding (`if let user = try? await fetchUser(id:)`). Callers that explicitly handle the nil case will fail to compile.
emitted.suggested_action: Revert the return type to `User?` to maintain backward compatibility, or introduce this as a new method with a different name (e.g., `fetchUserRequired(id:)`).

expected.rationale: Public method changed from User? to User — source-breaking change for callers. Per steering, source-breaking signature changes are blocker.

emitted.title: Public method returns non-optional String for potentially nil value
emitted.body: The method signature returns non-optional `String` but retrieves a value from `UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey:)` which returns `String?`. This API contract promises a `String` will always be available, but the underlying storage may not contain the key. Callers have no way to handle the absence of a username through the API contract.
emitted.suggested_action: Change the return type to `String?` to accurately represent that a username may not be present.

expected.rationale: currentUserName() returns non-optional String but the underlying UserDefaults.string(forKey:) is Optional<String>. The signature lies to callers about whether nil is possible. Should be -> String? or take a default.

emitted.title: New public method `currentUserName()` has no test coverage
emitted.body: The new public method `currentUserName()` is introduced without any corresponding test in the diff. This method reads from UserDefaults and force-unwraps the result, which are behaviors that should be tested to verify correct key usage and handling of missing values.
emitted.suggested_action: Add a test that verifies `currentUserName()` returns the expected value when the username key exists in UserDefaults.

expected.rationale: currentUserName() is a new public method introduced in this diff with no accompanying test. Per test_coverage.md, new public/open declarations without a test entry point in the diff are flagged at severity major.

emitted.title: Retain cycle: closure captures `self` strongly
emitted.body: The sink closure `{ event in self.handleEvent(event) }` captures `self` strongly. Since the cancellable is stored in `self.cancellables`, this creates a retain cycle: `self` → `cancellables` → `AnyCancellable` → closure → `self`. The subscription will never be deallocated.
emitted.suggested_action: Use `[weak self]` in the closure: `.sink { [weak self] event in self?.handleEvent(event) }`

expected.rationale: The .sink closure captures self strongly and is stored in cancellables, which is owned by self — a textbook Combine retain cycle. Per correctness.md blocker definition (`will crash, corrupt data, or violate a documented invariant`), a retain cycle leaks memory but does not crash; therefore severity is major, not blocker.
